intro: "Shadow" in Scripture carries both literal and theological freight. In its literal sense, shadow provides relief from the Palestinian sun and becomes a metaphor for divine protection: God shelters his people "under the shadow of his wings" (Ps. 17:8; 57:1; 91:1; cf. Ruth 2:12). The brevity of life is compared to a passing shadow (Job 14:2; Ps. 102:11; 144:4; Eccl. 6:12). Theologically, the OT's cultic system of priests, tabernacle, and sacrifices is described in the NT as a "shadow" (skia) of the heavenly realities and good things that have come in Christ (Col. 2:17; Heb. 8:5; 10:1) — a type or anticipatory image, not itself the substance. The contrast between shadow and substance (or body) is central to the Epistle to the Hebrews' argument for the superiority of Christ's priesthood and sacrifice. James 1:17 notes that with God "there is no variation or shadow due to change," affirming his immutability.
